quote: Okay. Totally understandable. So we, unfortunately, would have to just wait until you close on the March 23. So I can just give you a call then, and then we can get that estimate out to you and then look at getting the panels on the roof.
stewart_read: Emily's procedural read is partially correct — dq_rules.not_homeowner is a hard DQ, and she should not book a sit before Andy owns the home. The failure is not that she exited; it's that she exited without executing any button-up for the future appointment. Andy gave her a specific close date (March 23). The correct move was to lock a post-close callback: a specific date, a specific time, a text confirmation promise — the same button-up elements required for any booked appointment. Instead she issued a passive 'I'll call you back' with no anchor. Strategic read rejected: there is no evidence Emily was deferring to set up a later move — she simply stopped.
alternative_hypothesis: Emily deliberately kept the callback loose to give Andy flexibility around his close date, intending to call on March 23 and book then.
coaching_implication: Show Emily the button_up section. The coaching point is not 'you should have booked before he owned the home' — that would be wrong. The coaching point is: 'You had a specific close date and a warm buyer. You needed to lock March 24 or 25 as a callback appointment, confirm his number, and promise a text. You left with nothing anchored.' Role-play the post-close button-up on a pre-close lead.

quote: I just wanted to give you a head up. So what I'm doing right now is I'm actually buying a home there that home. I'm just closing out on March 23. So I'm just gonna kind of stop in that. I I don't own, like, as of right now, but on March 23, I should own it. So I'm trying to just find estimate first.
stewart_read: Andy is disclosing a significant life event — he's in the middle of a home purchase, not yet closed, and he's proactively flagging this so Emily doesn't feel misled. The slightly apologetic framing ('I'm just gonna kind of stop in that') signals he's offering a trust gesture, not an objection. Emily's response — 'Okay. Totally understandable.' — is the minimum acknowledgment. There is no empathy bridge: no recognition of the excitement of buying a new home, no connection between that life moment and why getting solar right from day one matters. The disclosure was a trust gift; Emily accepted it procedurally and moved to the DQ exit.
alternative_hypothesis: Emily's brevity was intentional — she didn't want to over-invest in a lead she couldn't book today.
coaching_implication: Per coaching_philosophy.empathy_on_vulnerability_disclosure, a life-event disclosure is a trust gift. The manager should ask Emily: 'What could you have said in 10 seconds that acknowledged what Andy is going through before you told him you couldn't help him today?' A suggested bridge (not a script): acknowledge the home purchase, connect it to why solar planning now makes sense, then pivot to locking the post-close appointment. The empathy moment and the button-up failure are linked — if Emily had engaged the disclosure, she would have had a natural opening to say 'let's make sure we're ready the day you close.'

quote: Do you guys have, like is there a, double money credit or anything like that, a military credit or anything that, the double money, you?
stewart_read: Andy is asking about incentives unprompted — this is the clearest buying signal in the call. He's already thinking about cost structure and wants to understand what he can get. Emily responds with a generic bill-swap pitch ('we do have this one incentive going on... bill swap program... no down payment, no upfront cost') rather than anchoring on his specific curiosity and using it to build toward a future appointment. The flip opportunity here: Andy's question about credits is an opening to say 'that's exactly what our specialist walks through — the full incentive picture for your specific home. Let's make sure we have that conversation the week you close.' Emily instead delivers a features monologue that doesn't advance the booking.
alternative_hypothesis: Emily's incentive pitch was a deliberate attempt to keep Andy engaged before the DQ was surfaced.
coaching_implication: Two coaching beats here. First, per verify.interest_in_solar, Andy's incentive question is the emotional anchor — Emily should have spent 30+ seconds exploring what's driving it (military background? cost anxiety? neighbor influence?) rather than pivoting to a generic pitch. Second, per coaching_philosophy.objection_inversion and analysis_directives.flip_opportunity_detection: Andy's cost curiosity was a flip opportunity. Suggested intent (no fabricated numbers): 'The incentive picture is exactly what our specialist maps out for your specific home — it's different for every property. That's the conversation worth having the week you close. Let's lock that in now.' The flip turns his curiosity into the reason to book the future appointment.

quote: So I mean, is there a way to, like, estimate Rampart? Like, how much does it usually cost, stuff like that?
stewart_read: Andy's cost question after the DQ exit is a second attempt to stay engaged — he's not ready to hang up. Emily's response ('each home definitely does differ, and so that's why we first have to accurately design an estimate') is technically correct per rebuttals.price_estimate_before_design and setter_scope_discipline, but it's deployed as a closing rationale rather than as a curiosity-creation move. She uses the 'we can't give a ballpark' logic to justify ending the call rather than to redirect toward booking the post-close appointment. The correct move: 'That's exactly why the specialist call is worth doing — they build the actual design for your home, not a generic number. Let's lock that for the week of March 23.'
alternative_hypothesis: Emily correctly declined to give a ballpark per the schema and was appropriately wrapping the call given the DQ.
coaching_implication: The manager should show Emily that the 'we can't give a ballpark' rebuttal is a redirect tool, not a call-ending tool. Per rebuttals.price_estimate_before_design, the canonical response redirects to the design appointment. Emily used it to close the conversation instead. Ask her: 'What was Andy telling you when he asked that question a second time?' The answer is: he wanted to stay engaged. That was the moment to lock the post-close appointment.

quote: Alright. Sounds good. So I just work with my design team to create free solar estimates for homeowners.
stewart_read: Emily opens with a generic design-team framing rather than the Tesla-certified intro. The schema's Tesla intro ('We're the certified solar installer for Tesla in Texas') is specifically designed to answer 'is this a scam?' in the first 30 seconds. Andy's warm response suggests he wasn't skeptical on this call, so the miss didn't cost energy here — but the pattern is worth flagging. On a less cooperative customer, skipping the legitimacy anchor in the intro is a recoverable-but-unnecessary hole.
alternative_hypothesis: Emily may have been on a non-Tesla lead source and correctly used the standard intro.
coaching_implication: Low urgency on this call given Andy's cooperative posture, but worth a quick check: was this a Tesla-sourced lead? If yes, Emily should default to the Tesla intro per intros.tesla. If no, the standard intro is correct and this is a non-issue. Manager should verify lead source before raising this in the 1-on-1.

quote: Oh, okay. Yeah. Totally understand. Well, so, actually, our company, we do have this one incentive going on. We're doing what's called a bill swap program. So that's just where we switch out your current bill for the electricity company with a more lower cheaper rate fixed rate bill.
stewart_read: Emily introduces the bill-swap concept but has never asked Andy what he's currently paying. The bill anchor — the schema's primary emotional lever — is deployed as a generic pitch rather than as a personalized flip. 'A more lower cheaper rate fixed rate bill' is abstract without Andy's actual number attached to it. Per coaching_philosophy.bill_anchoring, the capture-and-flip sequence requires knowing the bill first. Emily skipped the capture entirely and went straight to a features description.
alternative_hypothesis: Emily was responding to Andy's incentive question in real time and hadn't yet reached the bill-collection beat in the verify sequence.
coaching_implication: The manager should walk Emily through the capture-and-flip sequence per coaching_philosophy.bill_anchoring. The coaching question: 'When Andy asked about credits, what would have happened if you'd said: what are you paying right now on your electric bill? — and then used his number to make the bill-swap concrete?' The bill question would have served double duty: captured a required verify item AND given Emily the emotional anchor for the pitch. She did neither.
